swift_intro.title: Swift Overview
swift_intro.summary: A modern, type-safe language by Apple for iOS, macOS, server and more.
swift_intro.details:
Swift is a modern, safe, high-performance language designed for Apple platforms and server-side use. Practical checklist and deep-dive topics:

- Toolchains & environments: Xcode for Apple targets, Swift toolchain for Linux and CI. Manage multiple toolchains via xcode-select or swiftenv.
- Language core: value semantics (structs/enums), optionals, protocols, generics, protocol extensions and strong type system. Favor clear API boundaries and prefer immutability.
- Concurrency & safety: async/await, Task, TaskGroup and actors for structured concurrency and data race protection.
- Interop & migration: bridging with Objective-C, using @objc, dealing with Foundation types and memory semantics across language boundaries.
- Ecosystem & packaging: Swift Package Manager (SPM) for libraries/executables, SwiftLint/format for style, DocC for docs, and common CI patterns.

Study path: basics (syntax/types) → Optionals/Collections → Protocols & Generics → Concurrency → Tooling/Deployment. Keep a small sample app and iterate with tests and CI.
swift_intro.cheat:
// Quick commands
swift --version
swift build -c debug
swift build -c release
swift test
swift run <executable>

// Local dev tips
- Use `swift package generate-xcodeproj` only if older tooling; prefer Xcode's native SPM support.
- Use `swift build -c release -Xswiftc -Osize` for size-optimized builds.
- Keep Package.swift platforms minimal (e.g., iOS 14+) to reduce compatibility issues.

interview.a: A compiled, modern language by Apple for apps and server-side code.

interview.q: Why prefer Swift over Objective-C for new projects?
interview.a: Safer types, optionals, modern syntax and better ergonomics.
Structs/enums copied on assignment; they promote immutability.
Swift Package Manager for building and managing packages.
Binary compatibility across Swift versions on supported platforms.
Types that explicitly model possible absence of a value (T?).
Design approach using protocols and extensions to share behavior.
An inline, first-class function capturing its environment.
Use `swift test` or Xcode's test runner.
Protocol for easy encoding/decoding between types and formats like JSON.
iOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, and Linux for server-side Swift.
Safe access of properties/methods on optionals using `?.`.

interview.q: What is the REPL for Swift?
interview.a: An interactive prompt via `swift` for quick experiments.

interview.q: What are enums with associated values?
interview.a: Enums that can carry data per case, like ADTs.

interview.q: When to use classes vs structs?
interview.a: Use classes for identity/inheritance, structs for value semantics and immutability.
Compiler deduces types so many annotations are optional.
open, public, internal, fileprivate, private control visibility.

interview.q: What is the Swift evolution process?
interview.a: Community-driven proposal system for language changes.
They allow reusable, type-safe code for many types.

interview.q: What is the best way to learn Swift concurrency?
interview.a: Start with async/await, Tasks, TaskGroup and actors patterns.

swift_syntax.title: Syntax & Types
swift_syntax.summary: Basic syntax, optionals, pattern matching and control flow.
swift_syntax.details:
Essential syntax & typing patterns with pragmatic recommendations:

- Bindings: use `let` by default; use `var` only when mutation is necessary. Prefer small scopes for mutability.
- Optionals: prefer `guard let` for early exits, `if let` for local unwraps, `??` for defaults, and optional chaining `?.` for safe accesses. Avoid force-unwraps except in tests or when invariant is guaranteed.
- Control flow: use `switch` exhaustively with enums; prefer pattern matching and `where` clauses for clarity.
- Functions: annotate return types for public APIs; use default arguments, variadics and `inout` sparingly.
- Closures: annotate capture lists (`[weak self]`) explicitly when closures can outlive scope to avoid retain cycles.
- Small idioms: computed properties, property observers (willSet/didSet), tuples for small grouped returns and typealiases to improve readability.

Style tips: prefer clear names, keep functions < 30 lines, extract complex logic into small helpers, add unit tests for edge cases.

examples.code:
let a = 5 
var b = 10
let sum = a + b

interview.a: let declares a constant; var declares a mutable variable.

interview.q: How to safely unwrap an Optional?
interview.a: Use if let or guard let, optional chaining, or nil-coalescing.

interview.q: What is pattern matching in Swift?
interview.a: switch supports matching values, cases, ranges and where clauses.
{ (params) -> Return in body } or shorthand $0, $1.
A lightweight grouping of values, e.g., (Int, String).

interview.q: How to declare a function with default params?
interview.a: func f(x: Int = 0) { }
a ?? b returns a if not nil, otherwise b.

interview.q: How to create a computed property?
interview.a: var area: Double { width * height }
A function marked throws that can throw errors.

interview.q: How to mark a method mutating?
interview.a: Use mutating on struct methods that change self.
Alias a complex type for clarity.
Restricts visibility to the same file.

interview.q: How to use where in switch?
interview.a: case let x where x > 0: ...
A blueprint of methods/properties a type can conform to.

interview.q: How to create an enum with raw values?
interview.a: enum E: Int { case a = 1 }
A property initialized on first access.
When a function/type must work across multiple types safely.
willSet/didSet for reacting to property changes.
Provide default implementations for protocol methods.

interview.q: How to pattern-bind in for loops?
interview.a: for case let .some(x) in array { ... }

swift_tooling.title: SPM & Tooling
swift_tooling.summary: Swift Package Manager, Xcode, linters and formatters.
swift_tooling.details:
Tooling deep-dive and CI-ready practices:

- SPM: Package.swift defines products, targets and dependencies. Prefer semantic versioning for dependencies.
- Xcode: integrate SPM packages via File > Swift Packages for app projects; use Xcode Previews for UI iteration.
- Linters/Formatters: swiftlint for enforceable rules, swift-format for formatting. Add pre-commit hooks and CI steps to enforce.
- Editors: use sourcekit-lsp for code completion and diagnostics in editors like VSCode.
- CI: cache SPM artifacts (.build and .build/checkouts), run swift test and lint in separate stages, publish artifacts (binaries/archives) to package registries.

Packaging: provide clear README, license and example usage in packages. Use binary targets (XCFramework) for prebuilt libs when needed.
swift_tooling.cheat:
// SPM commands
swift package init --type library
swift build
swift test
swift run <target>

// CI tips
- Cache ~/.swiftpm and .build
- Use `swift test --parallel` for faster test runs
- Upload artifacts (dSYMs) for releases and crash symbolication

interview.a: The manifest file that defines package metadata, targets and dependencies.

interview.q: How to add a dependency with SPM?
interview.a: Add its Git URL and version to dependencies in Package.swift.

interview.q: How to run tests with SPM?
interview.a: swift test
A linter enforcing style and best practices.
Use swift-format or Xcode’s built-in formatter.

interview.q: How to run an executable target?
interview.a: swift run <target-name>

interview.q: What is a target in SPM?
interview.a: A build unit: library, executable or test target.

interview.q: How to generate Xcode project from SPM?
interview.a: Xcode integrates SPM directly; `swift package generate-xcodeproj` is legacy.

interview.q: How to pin versions in SPM?
interview.a: Specify exact version or range in Package.swift dependencies.
Prebuilt artifacts (XCFramework) referenced in a manifest.

interview.q: How to run tests in parallel?
interview.a: Use `swift test --parallel`.

interview.q: How to add resources to SPM target?
interview.a: Use `.process` resources in the target definition.
swift package show-dependencies

interview.q: How to integrate SPM in CI?
interview.a: Run swift build/test and cache .build and SPM caches.
Language server for Swift used by editors for code intelligence.

interview.q: How to debug SPM packages in Xcode?
interview.a: Add package to an Xcode project and set breakpoints in package source.
Platforms like iOS, macOS, tvOS with minimum versions.

interview.q: How to create an executable package?
interview.a: Define an executable target and run swift run.

interview.q: How to publish a Swift package?
interview.a: Tag a repository release and make sure Package.swift is correct.

interview.q: How to manage credentials in CI?
interview.a: Store tokens as secure environment variables or secrets.

swift_concurrency.title: Concurrency (async/await & actors)
swift_concurrency.summary: Structured concurrency, async/await, tasks, actors and MainActor.
swift_concurrency.details:
Concurrency best practices and anti-patterns:

- Structured concurrency: prefer Task and TaskGroup for scoping concurrent work; they provide automatic cancellation propagation.
- Actors: use actors to protect shared mutable state; avoid leaking actor-isolated data to non-isolated contexts.
- MainActor: annotate UI-touching code with `@MainActor` or use `MainActor.run` to schedule work on main thread.
- Continuations: wrap callback-based APIs with `withCheckedContinuation` or `withCheckedThrowingContinuation` for safe bridging to async/await.
- Cancellation: make long-running work cooperative—check `Task.isCancelled` regularly and release resources.
- Performance: avoid heavy CPU work on the actor; offload to detached tasks or worker pools.

Testing: write async XCTest cases and simulate cancellations/timeouts to ensure robustness.

interview.a: Language syntax to write asynchronous code that looks sequential.
A reference type that serializes access to its mutable state.
A unit of asynchronous work that can be awaited or cancelled.
Structured concurrency primitive to run multiple child tasks and collect results.
A global actor ensuring code runs on the main thread.
Call cancel() on the Task and check Task.isCancelled in the body.
Bridges to convert callback APIs into async/await using withCheckedContinuation.
Parent-child task hierarchy managing lifetimes and cancellations.

interview.q: When to use actor vs lock?
interview.a: Prefer actors for safety in Swift concurrency; locks are lower-level.
Creates a task not inheriting the current actor/priority context.
Use Task.sleep or implement timeout logic with TaskGroups and cancellation.
Actor code can be reentered across awaits; be mindful of invariants.
Use immutable values or actors for mutable shared state.
Use XCTest async tests and `await` the operations under test.
Tasks must check isCancelled and stop work cooperatively.

interview.q: How to use TaskGroup with error handling?
interview.a: Propagate errors and cancel remaining tasks if needed.

interview.q: How to bridge old APIs to async?
interview.a: Wrap callback-based APIs using continuations.
An actor whose instance is globally available to serialize access.
Avoid blocking high-priority tasks on low-priority work; use priorities carefully.

interview.q: Should heavy CPU work run in Tasks?
interview.a: CPU-bound work should run off the main actor, possibly in detached tasks or worker pools.

swift_protocols_generics.title: Protocols & Generics
swift_protocols_generics.summary: Protocol-oriented design, associated types and generic constraints.
swift_protocols_generics.details:
Protocol & generic design patterns:

- Protocols: define minimal interfaces; prefer small, focused protocols over large ones (Interface Segregation).
- Protocol extensions: provide shared default implementations but avoid hiding behavior that callers might expect to override.
- Associated types vs generics: use associatedtype in protocols where the conforming type decides the concrete type; use generics for functions and types that should be parameterized by callers.
- Opaque types: use `some Protocol` to hide concrete return types while preserving performance and type safety.
- Type erasure: implement type-erasure wrappers when different concrete generic types must be stored homogeneously (e.g., AnySequence, AnyPublisher).

Performance/compatibility: prefer concrete types on hot paths to allow specialization; measure impact before refactoring.

examples.code: protocol Repository { associatedtype Model; func get(id: Int) -> Model? }

interview.a: A blueprint of methods and properties a type can implement.
A placeholder type in a protocol that conforming types specify.
Hides the concrete return type while preserving performance.
Technique to hide concrete generic types behind a protocol box.
When you need type-safe reuse across different types.
Use T: Protocol or where clauses for complex constraints.
Combine multiple protocols with & in type annotations.

interview.q: How to avoid code duplication with generics?
interview.a: Extract generic helper functions or constrained protocol extensions.
Generic types conform only when their parameters meet constraints.
Provide makeIterator and implement IteratorProtocol with next().
Refers to the conforming type itself; affects type requirements.
Provide default implementations and shared behavior.
Alias for complex generic types to improve readability.

interview.q: How to debug complex generic errors?
interview.a: Simplify with typealias and add constraints incrementally.
When protocol requires reference semantics (use AnyObject).

interview.q: How to expose generic APIs in frameworks?
interview.a: Use protocols or opaque return types for stable ABI.
Swift generics are invariant; be careful with collections of supertypes/subtypes.

interview.q: How to store heterogeneous values conforming to protocol?
interview.a: Use type erasure wrappers.
Write tests with concrete types exercising generic paths.

interview.q: What are associatedtype requirements vs generics?
interview.a: associatedtype makes protocol require a type per-conformer; generics parameterize functions/types.

swift_error_handling.title: Error Handling
swift_error_handling.summary: Throwing functions, try/await, Result and recoverable errors.
swift_error_handling.details:
Errors & recoverability patterns:

- Use `throws` for recoverable problems; map low-level errors to domain-specific error enums.
- Distinguish between recoverable errors (use throws) and programmer errors (assert/fatalError in dev only).
- Use `try?` when failing silently is acceptable and `try!` only in tests or guarded contexts.
- Use Result<T,Error> for callback-based APIs, making success/failure explicit and composable.
- Logging & telemetry: record errors with context and user-safe messages; avoid leaking sensitive details.

Testing: assert thrown errors with `XCTAssertThrowsError` and assert error cases and messages.

examples.code:
enum MyError: Error { case notFound }
func load() throws -> String { throw MyError.notFound }

interview.q: How to declare a throwing function?
interview.a: Add `throws` to the function signature.
Converts thrown errors to an optional result (nil on error).
Represent success/failure in return values for callbacks.
Use `rethrows` for functions calling throwing closures.
It will crash on error; unsafe for recoverable conditions.

interview.q: How to provide user messages for errors?
interview.a: Map errors to localized user-facing strings, not raw error descriptions.

interview.q: How to add context to errors?
interview.a: Wrap lower-level errors into domain-specific errors with context.
Use XCTAssertThrowsError and verify error cases.

interview.q: How to handle errors in async functions?
interview.a: Use try await and catch in calling code.
Force-unwrap a throwing call; use only when certain no error will occur.
Map multiple underlying errors to a unified domain Error type.

interview.q: How to convert NSError to Swift Error?
interview.a: Bridge via NSError and wrap into Swift enums if needed.
For unrecoverable programmer errors during development.
Declare throws on functions and let callers handle or propagate.

interview.q: What are best practices for API error contracts?
interview.a: Define structured errors with codes and consistent payloads.
Inspect the thrown Error in catch and log stack/context.

interview.q: What is the cause of hidden errors?
interview.a: Using try? silently converts errors to nil; prefer explicit handling.

interview.q: How to recover from errors gracefully?
interview.a: Provide fallback values or retry strategies where appropriate.
Use assertions or fatalError during development to signal bugs.
Include cases, associated data and conformance to LocalizedError for messages.

swift_optionals_collections.title: Optionals & Collections
swift_optionals_collections.summary: Optionals, arrays, dictionaries, sets and sequence operations.
swift_optionals_collections.details:
Collections & optional handling — pragmatic patterns:

- Prefer strongly-typed collections ([T], [K:V], Set<T]); choose structures to match access patterns (ordered vs keyed vs unique).
- Use `compactMap` to transform while dropping nils; use `flatMap` for flattening nested arrays.
- Use `lazy` when chaining heavy transforms on big collections to avoid intermediate allocations.
- For streaming or very large datasets, use sequence generators or custom IteratorProtocol to process in chunks.
- Use `ArraySlice` to avoid copying when working with subranges; convert to Array when needing independent storage.

Performance: prefer in-place mutation (sort, removeAll(keepingCapacity:)) in hot loops and avoid frequent bridging to Objective-C collections.

interview.a: Transforms and removes nils from a sequence, returning non-optional results.

interview.q: When to use Set vs Array?
interview.a: Use Set for fast membership and uniqueness; Array for ordered elements.

interview.q: What is lazy evaluation for collections?
interview.a: Defers computation and avoids intermediate allocations when chaining operations.

interview.q: How to combine two arrays element-wise?
interview.a: Use zip(a,b).map { ... }

interview.q: How to get index and value while iterating?
interview.a: Use enumerated() to get (index, value) pairs.
A view into part of an array that shares storage with the original.

interview.q: How to group elements by key?
interview.a: Use Dictionary(grouping:by:) initializer.

interview.q: When to use Dictionary vs Map of arrays?
interview.a: Use Dictionary for direct keyed lookup; choose structure to match access patterns.

interview.q: How to remove duplicates but keep order?
interview.a: Track seen elements via Set while filtering the array.
Combine elements of a sequence into a single result.

interview.q: What is flatMap behaviour change to compactMap?
interview.a: flatMap used to flatten sequences; compactMap removes nils—API evolved.
Use a Set for O(1) membership checks.

interview.q: When to use lazy.map then first(where:)?
interview.a: To avoid creating full transformed arrays and stop early.

interview.q: How to sort an array in place?
interview.a: Use sort() for in-place, sorted() to return new array.
Stronger compile-time checks and clearer intent.

interview.q: How to convert Dictionary keys to array?
interview.a: Array(dict.keys) or dict.keys.map { $0 }

interview.q: How to remove elements while iterating?
interview.a: Use filter to create a new collection or iterate backward when mutating.
Collection supports multi-pass traversal and count; Sequence may be single-pass.
Use mapValues to transform values keeping keys.

interview.q: How to handle large datasets efficiently?
interview.a: Use lazy sequences, streaming and chunked processing to limit memory use.

swift_memory_arc.title: Memory Management & ARC
swift_memory_arc.summary: Reference counting, retain cycles, weak/unowned references and memory profiling.
swift_memory_arc.details:
Memory, ARC and leak avoidance:

- ARC applies to class instances. Use `weak`/`unowned` captures to avoid retain cycles in closures and delegate patterns.
- Prefer value types (structs/enums) to minimize ARC overhead and increase thread-safety.
- Use Instruments (Allocations, Leaks, Zombies) to detect leaks and unexpected retention.
- Use `@autoreleasepool` when bridging heavy Obj-C allocations inside loops to free memory earlier.
- For high-frequency allocations, reuse buffers (Data) or use `UnsafeMutableRawPointer` carefully in performance-critical sections.

Debugging: test deinit behavior with weak references and ensure resources are released in integration scenarios.

examples.code:
class A { var b: B? }
class B { weak var a: A? }

interview.a: Automatic Reference Counting manages object lifetimes by counting strong references.
Mutual strong references between objects or closures capturing self strongly.
Use weak when the referenced object can become nil and you want to avoid retaining it.
Use unowned when you are sure the reference will never be nil during use.

interview.q: How to break closure retain cycles?
interview.a: Capture self as [weak self] or [unowned self] in the closure.
Use Instruments Allocations and Leaks tools.
Mechanism to release autoreleased Objective-C objects earlier in loops.

interview.q: Why prefer structs to reduce ARC overhead?
interview.a: Structs are value types and avoid heap allocations for many cases.
Optimization where copies share storage until mutation occurs.
Use Allocations instrument and filter by responsible callers.
Excessive allocations and deallocations can cost CPU and memory churn.
Use Data with contiguous storage, reuse buffers and avoid frequent allocations.
Reattaching references during deinit; generally avoid and treat as bug.

interview.q: How to test for deinit in unit tests?
interview.a: Use weak references and assert they become nil after scope exit.

interview.q: Why avoid global singletons for large objects?
interview.a: They prolong lifetime and increase memory usage across app lifecycle.
Conversions between Swift and Foundation types can allocate; minimize in hot paths.

interview.q: How to use Instruments to find retain cycles?
interview.a: Use the Leaks and Cycles tools and check reference graph.

interview.q: When to use unowned optional pattern?
interview.a: Rare; prefer weak optionals to avoid crashes if lifecycle assumptions break.
Release caches on memory warnings and use lazy initializations.
Respond to system notifications by freeing caches and reducing allocations.

swift_server.title: Swift on Server (Vapor, Kitura)
swift_server.summary: Server-side Swift frameworks, routing, and deployment patterns.
swift_server.details:
Server-side Swift practical cheat-sheet:

- Frameworks: Vapor, Kitura and Perfect provide routing, middleware and ORM layers; pick one and standardize across services.
- Concurrency: use async handlers and non-blocking drivers for DB and HTTP clients; use connection pooling for database access.
- Configuration: store secrets in env variables or secret managers; avoid embedding secrets in images.
- Observability: expose /health and /metrics endpoints, emit structured logs and traces (OpenTelemetry) and integrate with APM.
- Deployment: build release binaries, containerize with multi-stage Dockerfiles and orchestrate with Kubernetes or systemd.

Security/ops: enable TLS, rotate keys, run security scans and enforce run-as-non-root policies in containers.

interview.a: A popular server-side Swift framework for web apps and APIs.
Define routes in configure or routes files and group middleware.
Use Fluent migrations to version schema changes.
Store secrets in env vars or a secret manager, not in source.
Run multiple stateless instances behind a load balancer and use external caches.
Expose /health and readiness endpoints for orchestration.
To reuse DB connections and reduce connection overhead.
Serve via a dedicated static server or CDN for performance.
Offload to background workers or job queues.
Use TLS, auth middleware and input validation.
Emit metrics and logs to monitoring systems (Prometheus, APM).
Use framework test helpers or integration tests with a test client.

interview.q: How to deploy server Swift apps?
interview.a: Build a release binary, containerize and deploy via orchestrator.
Stream uploads and store in object storage to avoid memory pressure.
Serve formats based on Accept header and requested content types.
Configure CORS middleware with allowed origins and methods.
Provide down migrations or migration rollback scripts with backups.
Restrict by network, auth and use strong credentials.

interview.q: What are best practices for logging?
interview.a: Structured logs, correlation IDs and avoid logging secrets.
Use rolling updates, draining connections and health checks.

swift_ui.title: SwiftUI & UI Patterns
swift_ui.summary: Declarative UI, state management, bindings and Combine integration.
swift_ui.details:
SwiftUI cheat and practical patterns:

- State management: use `@State` for view-local state, `@StateObject` for view-owned ObservableObjects, `@ObservedObject` for view observers and `@EnvironmentObject` for shared app state.
- Data flow: prefer unidirectional data flow; move business logic to view models and test them independently.
- Performance: avoid heavy computation in body; use `EquatableView`, `@ViewBuilder` and `@StateObject` to avoid frequent re-inits.
- Lifecycle: use `.task`, `.onAppear`, `.onDisappear` for async/side-effectful actions; keep UI code declarative.
- Interop: embed SwiftUI in UIKit with UIHostingController and integrate incrementally.

Testing: test view models and use Snapshot/UI tests for visual regressions.

examples.code: struct ContentView: View { @State var count = 0; var body: some View { Button("Tap \(count)") { count += 1 } } }

interview.a: Declarative UI framework for building interfaces across Apple platforms.
@State owns value in view; @Binding passes a reference to child views.
Creates and owns an ObservableObject for the view lifecycle.
Observes changes from an external ObservableObject but doesn't own it.

interview.q: How to perform side effects in SwiftUI?
interview.a: Use .task, .onAppear or call into view model async methods.
Use NavigationView and NavigationLink or the new navigationDestination APIs.
Test view models and use snapshot/UI tests for views.
Minimize work in body, use @ViewBuilder and EquatableView when appropriate.
Lift to view models and use enums to represent UI states.
Use publishers to feed view models and @Published to update views.
Use Bindings to connect UI controls to model state.
Use PreviewProvider to create various preview configurations.
Add accessibility modifiers and labels for UI elements.
Use LocalizedStringKey or Localizable.strings for translations.
Use withAnimation, .animation modifiers and transitions.

interview.q: How to embed SwiftUI in UIKit?
interview.a: Use UIHostingController to host SwiftUI views inside UIKit.
When platform APIs are missing or for complex legacy integrations.

interview.q: How to maintain state across app launches?
interview.a: Persist in storage and restore in scene lifecycle events.

interview.q: How to avoid view recreation costs?
interview.a: Use @StateObject and avoid recomputing heavy data in body.

interview.q: How to handle long lists efficiently?
interview.a: Use List with identifiable data and pagination or on-demand loading.

swift_testing.title: Testing & Tooling
swift_testing.summary: XCTest, mocking, testable architecture and CI integration.
swift_testing.details:
Testing & CI practical guide:

- Unit tests: isolate logic and use dependency injection to provide fakes/mocks for external dependencies.
- Async tests: leverage XCTest's async support and `await` calls directly inside test methods.
- Integration tests: run against ephemeral environments (Docker) for DB and external services; tear down reliably.
- CI: run lint, static analysis, unit tests and limited integration tests on PRs; full integration and performance tests on schedule.
- Flakiness: track flaky tests and quarantine until fixed; keep tests deterministic and small.

Coverage: measure but focus on meaningful, critical paths; code coverage alone is not a quality metric.

examples.code: final class MathTests: XCTestCase { func testAdd() { XCTAssertEqual(2+3,5) } }

interview.q: How to write unit tests in Swift?
interview.a: Create XCTestCase subclasses and add test methods starting with `test`.
Use async tests in XCTest and await async calls directly.
To replace real services with mocks/fakes and isolate units under test.
Abstract URLSession behind a protocol and provide a mock implementation.
Comparing UI output to a stored snapshot image or text.

interview.q: How to run tests in CI?
interview.a: Run `swift test` or xcodebuild test and collect results.
Remove timing dependencies and use deterministic fixtures.
Enable XCTest parallelization and distribute tests across CI agents.
Test logic in view models separately from views using injected dependencies.
Use ephemeral test DBs or transactions rolled back per test.
Enable code coverage flags and use coverage tools in CI.
Use deterministic schedules, stress tests and concurrency sanitizers.
Test-Driven Development: write tests before implementation to guide design.
Invoke executables and assert output and exit status.

interview.q: How to mock time in tests?
interview.a: Inject a clock abstraction and provide controllable test clocks.

interview.q: How to run a subset of tests locally?
interview.a: Use XCTest filters or run specific test targets.

interview.q: How to handle external service flakiness?
interview.a: Use mocks or local test doubles; run full integration tests in CI.
Altering code to ensure tests detect changes, measuring test quality.
Keep unit tests fast, move heavy integrations to separate suites.

interview.q: How to track flaky tests over time?
interview.a: Use CI analytics and quarantine flaky tests until fixed.

swift_debug.title: Debugging & Profiling
swift_debug.summary: LLDB, Instruments, logs and crash reporting.
swift_debug.details:
Debugging & profiling practices:

- LLDB: set breakpoints, use `po` to inspect objects and `bt` for backtraces. Use conditional breakpoints to reduce noise.
- Instruments: Time Profiler for CPU hotspots, Allocations for memory, Leaks for leaks, Network for traffic analysis and Energy for battery impact.
- Crash reporting: upload dSYMs to Sentry/Crashlytics for symbolication and actionable crash stacks.
- Sanitizers: enable Thread Sanitizer and Address Sanitizer in debug builds to find races and memory corruption.
- Logging: use os_log for structured logs and correlate traces using request IDs.

Operational: collect metrics and logs in central systems and retain for incident analysis.

examples.code:
lldb> breakpoint set --name viewDidLoad
lldb> run
lldb> po variable

interview.q: How to set breakpoints in LLDB?
interview.a: Use `breakpoint set --name <symbol>` or set via Xcode UI.
Tool to sample CPU usage and find hotspots.
Use Allocations and Leaks instruments.
Mapping addresses in crash reports to symbols using dSYMs.

interview.q: How to inspect variables in LLDB?
interview.a: Use `p` or `po` to print values and expressions.

interview.q: What is Address Sanitizer used for?
interview.a: Detect memory corruption and out-of-bounds accesses.
Use task-aware breakpoints and inspect task frames in the debugger.
Use crash reporters (Sentry, Crashlytics) and upload dSYMs.
Use Instruments Network instrument or proxies like Charles.

interview.q: How to reduce log overhead in production?
interview.a: Sample logs, use appropriate levels and redact sensitive data.
Tool to detect data races in concurrent code.

interview.q: How to debug SwiftUI rendering issues?
interview.a: Inspect view models and use Previews to replicate UI states.
Record allocation timeline and correlate to code paths.

interview.q: How to attach debugger to a process?
interview.a: Use lldb to attach to PID or run via Xcode.

interview.q: How to capture deterministic repro steps?
interview.a: Record inputs, environment and use logging to recreate bugs.

interview.q: How to debug memory leaks in mixed Obj-C/Swift code?
interview.a: Check bridging allocations and use Instruments to trace retain cycles.

interview.q: How to handle large trace data?
interview.a: Filter by symbol or timeframe and focus on relevant threads.

interview.q: How to test crash handling logic?
interview.a: Simulate errors and verify cleanup and recovery paths.

interview.q: How to inspect closures in LLDB?
interview.a: Print capture lists and closure context with `po`.
Profile the main thread in Instruments to find blocking work.

swift_performance.title: Performance & Optimization
swift_performance.summary: Profiling, algorithmic choices, inlining and value semantics.
swift_performance.details:
Performance & optimization checklist:

- Profile first: use Instruments and microbenchmarks on release builds before optimizing.
- Algorithmic focus: pick the right data structures and algorithms; often the biggest wins are algorithmic.
- Reduce allocations: prefer value types, reuse buffers and avoid bridging to Foundation in hot loops.
- Lazy sequences: use lazy chaining to avoid intermediate arrays when transforming large collections.
- Concurrency: parallelize CPU-bound tasks carefully using Tasks or Dispatch and avoid main thread blocking.
- Compiler flags: use -O / -Osize and enable Link Time Optimization (LTO) for release builds to improve performance.

Benchmarking: run stable benchmarks in CI and compare changes; keep measurement harness isolated from test noise.

interview.q: What is the first step before optimizing?
interview.a: Profile to identify real bottlenecks.

interview.q: Why prefer value types for performance?
interview.a: They can avoid heap allocations and be more cache-friendly.
Sharing storage until mutation triggers a copy for efficiency.
Use native Swift types and minimize conversions to Foundation types.
Only in critical hot paths or for interop where safe options are too costly.
Compiler optimization that can eliminate call overhead for small functions.

interview.q: How to reduce allocations in loops?
interview.a: Preallocate buffers and reuse objects where feasible.
Move heavy work off the main thread and precompute layouts.
Use keyed containers carefully and avoid unnecessary conversions.
Use release builds and Instruments or measurement frameworks.
A build optimization that performs cross-module inlining.

interview.q: How to use lazy to improve perf?
interview.a: Avoid intermediate arrays by deferring operations until needed.
For CPU-bound tasks with independent work using Tasks or Dispatch.

interview.q: How to measure cold vs warm performance?
interview.a: Test startup costs separately from steady-state performance.

interview.q: What is the cost of generic abstraction?
interview.a: Sometimes additional indirection; measure and specialize hot paths if needed.
Use Allocations instrument and track peaks and churn.
Avoid excessive thread creation and use task pools.
Batch requests, compress payloads and cache responses.
Benchmark before and after and run regression tests.

interview.q: When to accept trade-offs for readability?
interview.a: When the performance gain is marginal and clarity is more valuable.

swift_interop_objc.title: Interop with Objective-C
swift_interop_objc.summary: Bridging between Swift and Objective-C, annotations and memory semantics.
swift_interop_objc.details:
Objective-C interoperability cheats:

- Bridging: common Foundation types bridge automatically (String/NSString, Array/NSArray, Dictionary/NSDictionary, Date/NSDate, Data/NSData).
- Exposing Swift to Obj-C: inherit from NSObject and annotate methods/properties with @objc; keep API surface Obj-C friendly (no generics or Swift-only constructs directly).
- Selectors and KVO: use @objc and @objcMembers for selector-based callbacks and KVO-compatible properties; prefer Combine or Swift alternatives when possible.
- Memory: ARC is shared across Swift and Obj-C; watch closures and block capturing semantics.
- Wrappers: provide thin Obj-C-friendly wrappers for complex Swift APIs when necessary for older codebases.

Testing: exercise cross-boundary calls during integration tests to ensure correct bridging and memory behavior.

interview.q: How to expose Swift code to Objective-C?
interview.a: Inherit from NSObject and mark methods/properties with @objc.
String<->NSString, Array<->NSArray, Dictionary<->NSDictionary, Date<->NSDate, Data<->NSData.

interview.q: How to handle selectors in Swift?
interview.a: Use #selector(methodName) and annotate the method with @objc.

interview.q: How to convert NSError to Swift Error?
interview.a: Objective-C APIs with NSError** can be imported as throwing Swift functions.

interview.q: Can Swift generics be exposed to Obj-C?
interview.a: No; wrap generics in Obj-C-compatible classes or use type-erasure.
Annotates all members in a class to be exposed to Obj-C automatically.

interview.q: How to pass Swift closures to Obj-C blocks?
interview.a: Use @convention(block) when necessary and be mindful of memory semantics.

interview.q: What about memory across the boundary?
interview.a: ARC works across Swift and Obj-C; be mindful of retain cycles.

interview.q: How to import Obj-C frameworks into SPM?
interview.a: Use module maps or binary targets for C/Obj-C libraries.
Rename Obj-C APIs to more Swifty names when imported.

interview.q: How to expose constants from Obj-C?
interview.a: Declare extern constants in headers and they become available in Swift.

interview.q: How to bridge NSError domains to enums?
interview.a: Wrap NSError codes in Swift enums for typed error handling.
Enables Objective-C runtime dynamic dispatch and KVO compatibility.

interview.q: How to avoid fragile APIs when bridging?
interview.a: Keep Obj-C-facing APIs simple, typed and stable.
Add integration tests and ensure bridging headers are available to test targets.

interview.q: How to handle Objective-C selectors with Swift names?
interview.a: Use @objc(name) to set the selector explicitly.

interview.q: How to handle KVO in Swift?
interview.a: Use @objc dynamic and observe via key paths or use Combine where possible.

interview.q: How to wrap Swift-only constructs for Obj-C?
interview.a: Provide Objective-C-friendly wrapper classes or APIs.

interview.q: How to manage API evolution across boundaries?
interview.a: Keep Obj-C-visible APIs minimal and stable; deprecate carefully.

interview.q: Why avoid exposing too much to Obj-C?
interview.a: It makes APIs less Swifty and can limit modern language features.

swift_deployment.title: Build & Deployment
swift_deployment.summary: Release builds, code signing, App Store and server deployment patterns.
swift_deployment.details:
Build & deployment operations checklist:

- Apps: use Xcode Archives, manage provisioning profiles and ensure build signing is reproducible. Automate uploads to TestFlight and App Store Connect.
- Servers: build release binaries, create minimal container images (multi-stage Docker), and orchestrate with Kubernetes or systemd.
- CI/CD: run tests, lint, build artifacts, upload dSYMs, and publish signed artifacts. Use feature flag rollout and canary deployments for safety.
- Security: rotate keys, use secret stores and scan images for vulnerabilities; ensure runtime runs with least privileges.
- Observability: deploy health/readiness endpoints and integrate with monitoring/alerting prior to prod traffic shifts.

Rollback: have a tested rollback plan and automated steps to revert to previous stable artifacts if needed.
swift_deployment.cheat:
// Release build
swift build -c release
// Example Dockerfile (multi-stage)
FROM swift:5 as builder
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN swift build -c release
FROM debian:buster-slim
COPY --from=builder /src/.build/release/MyServer /usr/local/bin/
ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/MyServer"]

examples.code: swift build -c release ; docker build -t myserver .

interview.q: How to create a release build for SPM?
interview.a: swift build -c release

interview.q: How to archive an iOS app?
interview.a: Use Xcode Archives and upload via App Store Connect.
To symbolicate crash reports for readable stack traces.
Sign and submit to Apple notarization service, then staple if needed.

interview.q: How to containerize a Swift server app?
interview.a: Build the release binary and copy it into a minimal runtime image.
Ensures authenticity and integrity of distributed apps on Apple platforms.

interview.q: How to automate releases in CI?
interview.a: Use CI pipelines to build, test, sign and publish artifacts.

interview.q: How to manage secrets in CI?
interview.a: Use secret stores or encrypted CI variables, never commit secrets.

interview.q: How to run DB migrations on deploy?
interview.a: Run migrations as a separate step with backups and health checks.
Rolling updates or blue/green deploys ensuring continuous service availability.

interview.q: How to rollback a bad release?
interview.a: Use orchestrator rollbacks or redeploy the previous stable artifact.
Strip symbols, enable LTO and remove unused resources.

interview.q: How to sign CLI tools on macOS?
interview.a: Use codesign and notarization for distribution outside App Store.
Pin dependency versions and use deterministic build steps.
Build for each arch and push a manifest for multi-arch support.

interview.q: How to handle feature flags during rollout?
interview.a: Use remote config or flagging systems to toggle features per user.
Install on devices or use TestFlight for beta distribution.

interview.q: How to ensure security of build machines?
interview.a: Limit access, rotate keys and use ephemeral build agents when possible.
Use health checks, metrics, logs and alerting to detect regressions.
Use migrations with backward-compatible schema changes and phased deploys.

swift_best_practices.title: Best Practices & Patterns
swift_best_practices.summary: Idiomatic Swift, maintainable APIs, testing, and safety patterns.
swift_best_practices.details:
Best practices summary — keep this checklist in your repo:

- API design: be explicit with public types and annotate public APIs with clear documentation. Prefer stable interfaces and semantic versioning for libraries.
- Testing: keep unit tests fast and deterministic; use integration tests for external systems in CI.
- CI: enforce linting, formatting and static checks in CI; require green tests before merges.
- Security & privacy: validate input, handle secrets securely, and avoid logging sensitive data.
- Team practices: code reviews, architecture docs, and a lightweight onboarding guide for new contributors.

Maintainability: allocate time for refactors, track technical debt and keep examples and docs up-to-date.
swift_best_practices.cheat:
// Enforce style
swiftlint
swift-format format .
// CI baseline
- lint
- unit tests
- static analysis
- integration smoke tests

examples.code: protocol UserService { func get(id: Int) async throws -> User }

interview.a: Reduces bugs, simplifies reasoning and improves concurrency safety.
Composability and testing without deep inheritance.
Be explicit in types, document behavior, and minimize surface area.
Easier to test, reason about and reuse.
Track, prioritize and allocate time for refactors.
Use swiftlint and formatting steps in CI.
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH—increment MAJOR for breaking changes.
Use doc comments and publish docs using DocC or jazzy.
Inject dependencies and avoid global mutable state.
Refactor when adding features, fixing bugs, or when readability suffers.
Audit, pin versions and sandbox usage in CI.
Validate inputs, avoid force-unwraps, and fail early with clear errors.

interview.q: How to onboard new team members?
interview.a: Provide docs, sample apps and consistent patterns.
Deprecate gradually and follow semver for breaking changes.
Set least-privilege defaults and require explicit opt-in to relax security.
Design interfaces first then implement to match contracts.
Isolate mutable state, prefer actors and test concurrent scenarios.

interview.q: How to use code reviews effectively?
interview.a: Focus on correctness, readability, test coverage and architecture.

interview.q: How to measure success of practices?
interview.a: Track quality metrics, build times, test flakiness and incident counts.

interview.q: What is a good CI baseline?
interview.a: Run lint, static analysis, unit tests and basic integration checks on every PR.
